Maelezo kwa mhariri: Trakoma bado ni tatizo la afya ya umma katika nchi 41 (hadi Juni 2022) huku takribani watu milioni 125 wakiishi katika maeneo yanayohitaji afua dhidi ya ugonjwa huo. Trakoma hupatikana hasa katika maeneo ya maskini zaidi na ya vijijini zaidi ya Afrika, Amerika ya kati na Kusini, Asia, Pasifiki ya Magharibi na Mashariki ya kati. WHO kanda ya Afrika imeathiriwa kwa kiasi kikubwa na Trakoma huku watu milioni 105 wakiishi katika maeneo hatarishi, ambayo inawakilisha 84% ya wingi wa Trakoma Duniani. Trakoma ni chanzo kikuu cha maradhi ya upofu Duniani kote. Ugonjwa husababishwa kwa kuambukizwa na bakteria Clamydia Trachomatis. Maambukizi huama kutoka kwa mtu hadi kwa mtu kupitia vidole vilivyochafuliwa, Fomites na nzi ambao wamegusana na uchafu kutoka katika macho au pua ya mtu aliyeambukizwa.
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.” Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission. Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD. “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease. Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO commends Benin and Mali for eradicating trachoma as a public health problem. The WHO has certified Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in the WHO African region to achieve this important milestone. Other countries that have previously been certified by the WHO to eliminate trachoma include Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022), and Malawi (September 2022). "The WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali, and their network of national partners, on these significant achievements," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "Following the successes in Benin and Mali, trachoma remains a problem in 23 countries in the WHO African region, bringing us closer to the goal of eliminating trachoma as set out in the Global Elimination of trachoma by 2022 strategy. Worldwide, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been certified by the WHO to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem. These countries are Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, the Islamic Republic of Iran, the Lao People's Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu. In Benin and Mali, the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy has been implemented to eliminate trachoma with the support of the WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy includes surgery to repair trichiasis; antibiotics to clear infections; facial cleanliness; and environmental hygiene, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission. Through the Global Trachoma Programme, Pfizer is providing azithromycin for trachoma control programs that are implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma control with other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the national elimination program. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after Dracunculiasis (in 2009) and Gambian African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma surveys and surveillance, and has launched control programs to achieve elimination goals, despite challenges in the northern regions of the country and social and political instability in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which is now also part of a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD. "These are major public health achievements," said Dr. Ibrahima Soce Fall, Director of the WHO's Global NTD Programme. "Benin and Mali demonstrate how a strong political commitment, multi-sectoral collaboration, surveillance, and community engagement can work together to achieve elimination of diseases." Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma in recent years. The number of people in the WHO African region who require trachoma treatment has decreased by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million by June 2022. Notes to editors: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022), with an estimated 125 million people living in areas at risk of the disease. Trachoma is prevalent in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Latin America, Asia, the Western Pacific, and the Eastern Mediterranean. The WHO African region is particularly affected by trachoma, with an estimated 105 million people living in high-risk areas, representing 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is a major cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection occurs through direct contact with infected eyes or nose, or through contact with contaminated fingers, fomites, or insects.

translations.source: Mambo hatarishi ya kimazingira kwa maambukizi ya Trakoma ni pamoja na hali duni ya usafi, kaya zenye msongamano mkubwa wa watu, kutopata maji ya kutosha au matumizi ya vifaa vya usafi wa mazingira. Mnamo 1996, WHO ilizindua muungano wa WHO wa kutokomeza Trakoma Duniani kufikia mwaka 2020 (GET 2020). Pamoja na washirika wengine katika muungano, WHO inaunga mkono utekelezaji wa mkakati SAFE nchini na uimarishaji wa uwezo wa kitaifa kupitia tathmini ya magonjwa, usimamiaji, ufuatiliaji, tathmini ya mradi na uhamasishaji wa rasilimali unaochangia kutokomeza Trakoma kama tatizo la afya ya umma. Muongozo, ulioidhinishwa na baraza la afya Duniani mwaka 2020 kupitia uamuzi wake wa 73(33), inaweka 2030 kama tarehe mpya inayolengwa ya kutokomezwa Duniani.
translations.reference: Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.
translations.hypothesis: Environmental factors that contribute to the spread of cholera include poor sanitation, densely populated areas, lack of access to safe water, and inadequate use of sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Global Task to Eliminate Cholera by 2020 (GET 2020). In collaboration with other partners, WHO supports the implementation of the SAFE strategy in countries and strengthens national capacity through disease surveillance, management, monitoring, project evaluation, and resource mobilization to eliminate cholera as a public health problem. The guidance, approved by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through resolution 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target year for global elimination.

translations.source: Watoto milioni 152 walizaliwa njiti katika muongo uliopita Kati ya watoto 10 wanaozaliwa, 1 ni njiti - na kila sekunde 40, mtoto 1 kati ya hao hufariki Viwango vya kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati havijabadilika katika muongo mmoja uliopita katika eneo lolote la Dunia. Athari za migogoro, mabadiliko ya hali ya hewa, UVIKO-19 zinaongeza hatari kwa wanawake na watoto kila mahali. Hatua zinahitajika haraka ili kuboresha uzuiaji wa kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, pamoja na utunzaji bora kwa watoto walioathiriwa na familia zao. Inakadiriwa watoto milioni 13.4 walizaliwa Njiti mwaka 2020, na karibu milioni 1 wakifa kutokana na matatizo ya unjiti, kulingana na ripoti mpya iliyotolewa leo na mashirika ya umoja wa mataifa. Hii ni sawa na mtoto 1 kati ya 10 aliyezaliwa mapema (kabla ya wiki 37 za ujauzito) Duniani kote. Alizaliwa hivi karibuni: Muongo wa hatua juu ya kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, iliyotolewa na shirika la Afya Duniani (WHO) na shirika la Umoja wa mataifa la kuhudumia watoto (UNICEF) pamoja na PMNCH - muungano mkubwa zaidi Duniani wa wanawake, watoto, na vijana, inapiga kengele kwenye "Dharura ya kimya kimya" ya kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, ambayo haijatambuliwa kwa muda mrefu katika kiwango na ukali wake, ambayo inazuia maendeleo katika kuboresha afya na maisha ya watoto. Ripoti hiyo inajumuisha makadirio yaliyosasishwa kutoka WHO na UNICEF, iliyotayarishwa na shule ya London ya usafi na madawa ya kitropiki, kuhusu kuenea kwa watoto wanaozaliwa kabla ya wakati. Kwa ujumla, ripoti inagundua kuwa viwango vya kuzaliwa Njiti havijabadilika katika eneo lolote Duniani katika muongo mmoja uliopita, na watoto milioni 152 walio katika mazingira magumu walizaliwa hivi karibuni kutoka 2010 hadi 2020. Kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati sasa ndio sababu kuu ya vifo vya watoto, ikichukua zaidi ya 1 kati ya vifo 5 vya watoto vinavyotekea kabla ya miaka 5 ya kuzaliwa. Waathirika wa kuzaliwa Njiti hukabiliwa na matokeo ya afya ya maisha yote, na kuongezeka uwezekano wa ulemavu na uchelewaji wa maendeleo. Mapengo ya kuishi kwa kanda, kipato, rangi Kutokana na ripoti ya kihistoria kuhusu mada mnamo 2012, ripoti hii mpya ya "Muongozo" wa kuzaliwa hivi karibuni inatoa muhtasari wa kina wa kuenea kwa kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati na athari zake kwa wanawake, familia, jamii na uchumi. Mara nyingi mno, mahali ambapo watoto huzaliwa huamua kama wataishi. Ripoti hiyo inabainisha kuwa ni mtoto 1 kati ya 10 wanaozaliwa mapema kabla ya wakati (chini ya wiki 28) wanaishi katika nchi zenye kipato cha chini, ikilinganishwa na zaidi ya 9 kati ya 10 katika nchi zenye kipato cha juu. Pengo la ukosefu wa usawa kuhusiana na rangi, kabila, mapato, ufikiaji wa huduma bora huamua uwezekano wa kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, kifo, na ulemavu, hata katika nchi zenye kipato cha juu. Kusini mwa Asia na Afrika kusini mwa jangwa la Sahara ndizo zinazo viwango vya juu zaidi vya kuzaliwa Njiti, na watoto wachanga katika maeneo haya wanakabiliwa na hatari kubwa zaidi ya vifo. Kwa pamoja, maeneo haya mawili yanachaangia zaidi ya 65% ya watoto wanaozaliwa kabla ya wakati wao kukamilika Duniani. Pia ripoti hiyo inaangazia kwamba athari za migogoro, mabadiliko ya hali ya hewa, na uharibifu wa mazingira, UVIKO-19, na kupanda kwa gharama za maisha kunaongeza hatari kwa wanawake na watoto kila mahali. Kwa mfano, uchafuzi wa hewa unakadiriwa kuchangia watoto milioni 6 wanaozaliwa Njiti kila mwaka. Karibu mtoto 1 kati ya 10 anayezaliwa kabla ya wakati analiwa katika nchi 10 dhaifu zaidi zilizoathiriwa na majanga ya kibinadamu, kulingana na uchambuzi mpya katika ripoti hiyo. Hatari za afya ya Uzazi, kama vile mimba za utotoni na pre-eclampsia, zinahusishwa kwa karibu na kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati. Hii inasisitiza haya ya kuhakikisha upatikanaji wa huduma za afya ya ngono na uzazi, ikiwa ni pamoja na upangaji uzazi bora, na utunzaji wa hali ya juu wakati wa ujauzito na wakati wa kujifungua. Ajenda ya utekelezaji: uwekezaji zaidi wa nchi na harakati zinazoongozwa na wazazi
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020. Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive. The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere. For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism
translations.hypothesis: Over the past decade, an estimated 152 million babies have been born prematurely. Of these, 1 in 10 babies is born prematurely – and every 40 seconds, 1 baby dies as a result. Premature birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any part of the world. The effects of conflict, climate change, and the COVID-19 pandemic are increasing the risk for women and children everywhere. Urgent action is needed to improve the prevention of premature birth, as well as the care of babies who are born prematurely and their families. An estimated 13.4 million babies were born prematurely in 2020, and about 1 million babies died as a result of premature birth, according to a new report released today by the United Nations. This is equivalent to 1 in 10 babies born prematurely worldwide. Recent developments: A new global strategy on premature birth, developed by the World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF, along with PMNCH – the largest global network of women, children, and young people – is calling for a "silent emergency" of premature birth to be addressed. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared by the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of premature birth. Overall, the report shows that premature birth rates have not changed in any part of the world in the past decade, and that 152 million babies have been born prematurely from 2010 to 2020. Premature birth is now the leading cause of child mortality, accounting for more than 1 in 5 deaths of children under 5. Babies born prematurely are at risk of a range of health problems, including respiratory distress, infections, and developmental delays. The risk of premature birth varies by region, income level, and race. In low-income countries, the risk of premature birth is higher than in high-income countries. The risk of premature birth is also higher for women who are young, have multiple pregnancies, or have pre-existing health conditions. The regions with the highest rates of premature birth are South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, where many babies are born prematurely and have a higher risk of dying. These two regions account for more than 65% of all premature births worldwide. The report also highlights the impact of conflict, climate change, and environmental degradation on women and children. For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million premature births each year. According to a new analysis in the report, 1 in 10 babies born prematurely dies in the 10 countries most affected by humanitarian crises. The risk of premature birth is also associated with other health problems, such as teenage pregnancy and pre-eclampsia. This highlights the importance of ensuring access to sexual and reproductive health services, including family planning and high-quality care during pregnancy and childbirth.

translations.source: Pia muuongo uliopita umeshuhudia ukuaji wa harakati wa jamii juu ya kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati na kuzuia uzazi, unaoendeshwa na mitandao ya wazazi, wataalamu wa afya, wasomi, Asasi za kiraia, na wengine. Kote ulimwenguni, Makundi ya familia zilizoathiriwa za kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati wao wamekuwa mstari wa mbele kutetea upatikanaji wa matunzo bora na mabadiliko ya sera na kusaidia familia nyingine. Kuelekea kongamano la kimataifa la afya ya mama wajawazito, huko Cape Town, Afrika Kusini, kuanzia tarehe 8-11 Mei, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA na PMMNCH wanatoa wito wa kuchukua hatua zifuatazo ili kuboresha huduma kwa wanawake na watoto wachanga na kupunguza hatari zinazotokana na kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati: Ongezeko la uwekezaji: Kuhamasisha rasilimali za kimataifa na za ndani ili kuboresha afya ya uzazi na watoto wachanga, kuhakikisha utunzaji wa hali ya juu unatolewa wakati na mahali unapohitajika. Kuharakisha utekelezaji: Kufikia malengo ya nchi kwa ajili ya maendeleo kupitia sera zilizowekwa za kitaifa za utunzaji wa uzazi na watoto wachanga. Ushirikiano katika sekta zote: Kukuza elimu kupitia mzunguko wa maisha; kusaidia uwekezaji mzuri wa kiuchumi; ufadhili wa pamoja katika sekta zote; kuimarisha majibu ya kukabiliana na hali ya hewa katika kipindi chote cha maisha; na kuendeleza uratibu na uthabiti wa mifumo ya dharura. Uvumbuzi unaoendeshwa ndani ya nchi: Kuwekeza katika uvumbuzi na utafiti unaoongozwa na ndani ili kusaidia uboreshaji wa ubora wa utunzaji na usawa katika ufikiaji. Nukuu: Profesa Joy Lawn, Shule ya London ya usafi na Tiba ya kitropiki Kiongozi mwenza, Alizaliwa hivi karibuni: Muongo wa hatua juu ya kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati (2023) na kuzaliwa hivi karibuni (2012) "Ripoti hii mpya inaonyesha kuwa gharama ya kutochukua hatua katika muongo uliopita ilikuwa watoto milioni 152 walizaliwa mapema sana. Wakati baadhi ya maeneo yameathiriwa zaidi, uzazi wa mapema unatishia maendeleo ya kiafya katika kila nchi. Uwekezaji mkubwa katika utunzaji wa watoto wachanga walio katika mazingira magumu unaweza kuokoa mamilioni ya familia kutokana na huzuni. Pia kazi zaidi inahitajika ili kuzuia kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, ambayo pia itaboresha maendeleo katika kupunguza vifo vya uzazi na vifo vya watoto wachanga. Kwa pamoja kauli mbizi hizi za kuzuia na kutunzaa kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati zitazalisha watu binafsi na jamii zenye afya zaidi ili kuleta maendeleo ya kiuchumi na kijamii. Kizazi chetu kijacho kinatutegemea sisi sote kuchukua hatua sasa -- uwekezaji unaweza usiwe mdogo lakini faida ya uwekezaji huu itakuwa kubwa kwa nchi" Dk Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon mwandishi mwenza na kiongozi wa kitaifa wa Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) "Sasa hakuna kisingizio cha kunyamaza. Sasa tuna mikononi mwetu vifaa na maarifa ya kubadilisha matokeo kwa watoto wetu wachanga walio katika mazingira magumu zaidi. Najivunia kuona serikali yangu sasa inawekeza na kujitolea utekelezaji hata maeneo ya vijijini. Muongo ujao uanaweza na lazima uwe tofauti kwa wale wanaokabiliwa na kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati hapa na kila mahali" Anshu Banerjee, mkurugenzi wa afya ya uzazi, watoto wachanga, watoto na vijana ndani ya WHO "Kuhakikisha huduma bora kwa watoto hawa wadogo zaidi, walio katika mazingira magumu zaidi na familia zao ni muhimu sana kwa kuboresha afya na maisha ya mtoto. Pia maendeleo yanahitajika ili kusaidia kuzuia uzazi kabla ya wakati -- hii inamaana kwamba kila mwanamke lazima aweze kupata huduma bora za afya kabla na wakati wa ujauzito ili kutambua na kudhibiti hatari" Steven Lauwerier, mkurugenzi wa afya (a.i.), UNICEF "Baada ya kila kifo cha mapema ni njia ya kupotez na kuvunjika moyo.
translations.reference: The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care. Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon. While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country" Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon co-author and national lead of Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) “There is now no excuse for silence. We now have in our hands the devices and the knowledge to change outcomes for our most vulnerable newborns. I am proud to see my government now investing and committed to implementation across even rural areas. The next decade can and must be different for those facing preterm birth here and everywhere.” Dr Anshu Banerjee, Director for Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at WHO “Ensuring quality care for these tiniest, most vulnerable babies and their families is absolutely imperative for improving child health and survival. Progress is also needed to help prevent preterm births -- this means every woman must be able to access quality health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks.” Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.), UNICEF “After every preterm death is a trail of loss and heartbreak.
translations.hypothesis: The recent report highlights the growing movement to address premature births and stillbirths, driven by networks of parents, healthcare professionals, academics, and civil society organizations. Across the globe, families affected by premature births are advocating for improved care and policy changes. In preparation for the International Maternal and Child Health Conference in Cape Town, South Africa, from May 8-11, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, and PMMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for mothers and newborns and reduce the risks associated with premature births: Increased investment: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to improve reproductive and child health, ensuring that high-quality care is available when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Achieving national goals for maternal and child health through the implementation of existing national policies. Collaboration across sectors: Promoting education through community outreach; supporting sustainable economic development; joint funding in all sectors; strengthening responses to climate change throughout the life course; and fostering coordination and resilience in emergency response. Domestic innovation: Investing in locally-led innovation and research to improve the quality and equity of care. Quotes: Professor Joy Lawn, Director, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, Co-chair of the Global Strategy on Newborn Don't miss this opportunity to learn more about premature births and stillbirths and to connect with other experts in the field.

translations.source: Licha ya maendele mengi ambayo ulimwengu umefanya katika muongo mmoja uliopita, hataujafanya maendeleo yoyote katika kupunguza idadi ya watoto wadogo wanaozaliwa mapema au kuepusha hatari ya kifo chao. Uhuru ni mbaya. Ni wakati wa kuboresha upatikanaji wa huduma kwa mama wajawazito na watoto wachanga wanaozaliwa kabla ya wakati na kuhakikisha kila mtoto anapata mwanzo mzuri na kustawi maishani" Helga Fogstad, mkurugenzi mtendaji, PMNCH "Kuzaliwa mapema kunasisitiza kwa nini ni lazima kuongeza uwekezaji na uwajibikaji kwa kuzaliwa kabla ya wakati, sababu kubwa zaidi ya vifo vya watoto chini ya umri wa miaka mitano, Duniani. Maendeleo yanadorora kwa afya ya uzazi na watoto wachanga, vilevile kuzuia watoto wanaokufa, na sasa yamerejeshwa nyuma zaidi kupitia mchanganyiko mbaya wa UVIKO_19, mabadiliko ya hewa, migogoro inayoongezeka na kupanda kwa gharama za maisha. "Kwa kufanya kazi pamoja kwa ushirikiano- serikali, wafadhili, sekta binafsi, asasi za kiraia, wazazi, na wataalamu wa afya - tunaweza kupinga kengele kuhusu "Dharura hii ya kimya", na kuleta jitihada za kuzuia na matunzo mbele ya afya ya kitaifa na juhudi za maendeleo, kujenga mtaji wa watu kwa kusaidia familia, jamii na uchumi kila mahali". "Gabriela na Jerome Foster, Las Vegaz, Marekani: wazazi wa Janel Foster, na waanzilishi wa Wakafu wa Jalen Gift. "Wakfu wetu ulianzishwa baada ya kufiwa na mtoto wetu wa kiume, Jalen Foster, ambae alijifungua mtoto Njiti akiwa na wiki 27 kutokana na kutokea kwa Bakteria wanaojulikana kama Streptococcus kundi B. Kumpoteza mtoto wetu wa kiume kulituumiza sana, na hakukuwa na usaidizi katika jamii na ndipo tulipoamua kutoa mahali pa faraja na matumaini kwa familia ambazo zimepoteza mtoto. Hatukutaka familia nyingine zijisikie kama sisi- peke yao. Kwa hiyo, tulianzisha Wakfu wa Jalen Gift ili kusaidia huduma nyingi bila malipo, kama vile picha, usaidizi wa huzuni na usaidizi kuelekea huduma za ukumbusho" *** Pakua Born too Soon rasilimali za media
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five. Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby. We didn’t want other families to feel like we did – alone. So, we started Jalen’s Gift Foundation to help other families going through a loss, to provide multiple free services, such as photograph, grief support and help towards memorial services.” ***Download the Born too soon media resources
translations.hypothesis: Despite the significant progress the world has made in the past decade, we have not yet made sufficient progress in reducing the number of babies born prematurely or preventing their deaths. Premature birth is a serious problem. It is time to improve access to care for pregnant women and newborns, and to ensure that every child has a healthy start in life. Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH "Premature birth highlights the urgent need to increase investment and accountability in preventing preterm birth, the leading cause of child deaths globally. Progress in maternal and child health, including preventing preterm birth, has stalled, and is now being reversed by a combination of factors, including the COVID-19 pandemic, climate change, increasing conflict, and rising costs of living. "By working together – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and healthcare professionals – we can turn the tide on this "silent emergency" and bring prevention and treatment efforts forward in national health and development agendas, building a stronger future for families, communities, and economies everywhere." Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, USA: Parents of Janel Foster, and founders of the Jalen Gift Foundation. "Our foundation was established after the tragic death of our son, Jalen Foster, who was born prematurely at 27 weeks due to an infection caused by Streptococcus B. Losing our son was a devastating experience, and we realized that there was a need for support and resources for other families who had experienced similar losses. We didn't want other families to feel as alone as we did. That's why we created the Jalen Gift Foundation to provide a range of free services, such as bereavement support, counseling, and resources for remembrance." *** Download Born too Soon media resources

translations.source: UAE na WHO zinapeleka vifaa muhimu vya matibabu nchini sudani Umoja wa falme za kiarabu (UAE) na shirika la afya Duniani zimewasilisha tani 30 za vifaa vya dharura vya matibabu nchini sudani leo. Ndege iliyobeba vifaa vya matibabu ya majeraha, upasuaji wa dharura, dawa muhimu iliwasili katika uwanja wa ndege wa Port sudan mapema leo asubuhi. Shehena hiyo, yenye thamani ya Dola za kimarekani 444,000, ni ya kwanza ambayo WHO imeweza kuwasilisha kwa ndege hadi Sudani tangu kuzuka kwa mzozo huo. WHO ilisambaza vifaa kwa vituo vya afya kabla ya kuongezeka kwa mgogoro. Viliishiwa baada ya siku chache kutokana idadi ya waliojeruhiwa. Kutumwa kwa ndege hiyo kunakuja kama sehemu ya juhudi za umoja wa falme za kiarabu kuwaunga mkono watu wa Sudani na kuakisi uhusiano wake wa kina na Sudani, hasa kwa kuzingatia hali ya sasa ya nchi hiyo. Pia misaada hiyo ni upanuzi wa maono ya kibinadamu ya UAE na inasisitiza uhusiano wake na nchi nyingie na kulingana na udugu wa kibinadamu na kujitolea kwake kusaidia wengine wakati wa majanga na hali za Dharura. Dk Reem binti Ebrahim Al hashimy, waziri wa nchi na ushirikiano wa kimataifa, alisema kuwa UAE "inaendelea kufanya kazi kwa karibu na shirika la afya Duniani ili kuhakikisha kupelekwa kwa shughuli muhimu za usafirishaji kutuma msaada wa haraka wa matibabu na chakula kusaisaidia kukabiliana na mzozo wa sasa nchini sudani. Sambamba na kutoa wito kwa pande zote za mzozo wa sasa kusitisha mara moja uhasama na kupunguza hali inayosababisha mateso yasiyoelezeka kwa watu wa Sudani, UAE iko imara katika ahadi yake ya kutoa misaada ya usaidizi kwa nchi wakati wa mahitaji. Umoja wa falme za kiarabu unalenga hasa kutoa mahitaji ya makundi yaliyo hatarini zaidi yaliyoathiriwa na hali nchini Sudani, hususani wagonjwa, watoto, wazee na wanawake ambao wapo katika hatari zaidi kutokana na mzozo unaoendelea - na safari hizi za ndege moja kwa moja zitatua mapengo makubwa zaidi katika utoaji wa matibabu na chakula. Maadili ya kibinadmu yaliyoshikiliwa kwa kina ya UAE yamemaanisha kwamba imehakikisha kuwa imeendelea kuwasiliana na ulimwengu kujitolea kwake kwa nguvu na kwa kudumu katika kuimarisha amani, usalama na utulivu, sio tu kikanda, lakini pia Duniani kote, wakati wa pamoja na washirika wake na jumuia ya kimataifa, nchi imendelea kusaidia watu wa Sudani wakati wa machafuko" vifaa ni pamoja na kiwewe cha kutosha, vifaa vya upasuaji wa Dharura, na dawa muhimu kufikia mara moja watu 165,000 ambao wanahitaji sana msaada wa kibinadamu. Vituo vya afya kote nchini vinaripoti kuwa vimekosa vifaa vya msingi vya afya na kwamba Bohari ya Taifa ya Dawa haifikii tena kutokana na hali ya usalama. Miongoni mwa msaada mwingine, WHO inapeleka wataalamu wawili wa vifaa vya Dharura wanaosafiri na mizigo ili kuhakikisha kwamba vifaa hivi vinasambazwa mara moja kwa vituo vikuu 13 vya afya ili kusaidia wahudumu wa afya na kupanua huduma kwa wale wanaohitaji. WHO ina tani nyingine 30 za vifaa kwa ajili ya Malaria na magonjwa yasiyoambukiza kama vile Kisukari na Shikinizo la damu, hali ambayo inaweza kuwa mbaya kama haitibiwi. Mifumo hii na takribani 23,000 ya mifuko ya damu inasomwa ndani ya kituo cha vifaa cha kimataifa cha WHO katika jiji la kimataifa la kibinadamu, na WHO kwa sasa inachunguza uwezekano wote wa kuwasilisha vifaa hivi nchini Sudani haraka iwezekanavyo kwa ushirikiano na wizara ya afya ya Sudani. Dk Michael Ryan, mkurugenzi mtendaji, wa mpango wa Dharura ya afya wa WHO, alisema, "kituo cha usafirishaji cha WHO huko UAE kimekuwa kitovu cha muitiko wa haraka wa matukio ya Dharura ulimwenguni. Husaidia zaidi ya nchi 140 katika maeneo yote 6 ya kijografia ya WHO, kitovu hutoa njia ya kuokoa maisha kwa nchi wanachama zinazokabiliwa na Dharura za kiafya zinazotokana na milipuko ya magonjwa, majanga kama vile mafuriko na matetemeko ya ardhi, na athari za migogoro kwenye mifumo ya afya. Msaada unaotolewa na UAE huwezesha WHO kuwa mstari wa mbele katika majibu haya ya Dharura ya afya- kutoa msaada wa matibabu wa kibingwa kwa wale wanaohitaji popote na wakati wowote inapohitajika. Kama sehemu ya juhudi za kuimarisha usanifu wa kimataifa kwa ajili ya maandalizi ya Dharura ya kiafya, muitikio, na ustahimilivu UAE, WHO, na washirika wanafanya kazi bega kwa bega kupeana vifaa ili kuwafikia waliohatarini zaidi Duniani katika wakati wao wa uhitaji mkubwa" Kwa upande wake, Dk Ahmed Al- Mandhari, mkurugenzi wa WHO kanda ya mashariki ya Mediterania, alisema, "shirika la afya Duniani linaendelea kuratibu na mamlaka ya afya ya Sudani kuwasilisha vifaa muhimu vya afya kupitia njia zote zinazopatikana. Kwa sababu ya mzozo, hospitali, maduka ya dawa na vituo vya huduma ya afya ya msingi vimekosa dawa muhimu, Dawa za kuokoa maisha. Kuwasili kwa tani 30 za ziada za vifaa vya kiwewe na upasuaji wa Dharura vilevile dawa muhimu kutawezesha WHO kusaidia vituo vikuu 13 vya afya na kuanzisha upya huduma za afya kwa wale wanaohitajika. Kituo chetu cha usafirishaji cha kimataifa huko Dubai kina jukumu muhimu katika kuhakikisha usambazaji usiokatizwa wa bidhaa za afya na tunashukuru kwa usaidizi unaotolewa na umoja wa falme za kiarabu kuwezesha safari yetu ya kwanza ya kukodi ya vifaa hivi vinavyohitajika sana.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict. These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need. The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need. WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed. As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies.
translations.hypothesis: The UAE and the World Health Organization (WHO) have delivered 30 metric tons of essential medical supplies to Sudan today. The shipment, which includes trauma and emergency surgery supplies, as well as essential medicines, arrived at Port Sudan airport earlier this morning. The delivery, worth $444,000, is the first shipment of this kind to reach Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. The WHO has been distributing the supplies to health facilities before the escalation of the conflict. The shipment is intended to help address the needs of the affected population, who have been facing shortages of essential medical supplies. The UAE has also pledged to provide further assistance to Sudan, and the delivery of these supplies is part of that commitment. The UAE is working closely with the WHO to ensure that essential medical supplies and food are delivered to those in need. In addition to the delivery of medical supplies, the WHO is also providing two emergency medical personnel to help distribute the supplies and provide medical assistance to those in need. The WHO is also providing 30 metric tons of malaria and other essential medicines, which are needed to treat a range of diseases. The UAE has also pledged to continue to support Sudan, and the delivery of these supplies is just one part of that commitment.

translations.source: Ni msaada kama huu ambao unaonyesha kwa vitendo dira yetu ya kikanda ya afya kwa wote, na wote" Misaada ya matibabu iliyotolewa na shirika la afya Duniani kupita UAE ilichangia takribani 55% ya jumla ya vifaa vya matibabu vilivyotolewa kwa Sudani kutoka nje ya nchi mwaka 2022, ikionyesha nafasi ya UAE kama kitovu cha usaidizi wa kimataifa wa kibinadamu.
translations.reference: It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis: This kind of support clearly demonstrates our regional health priorities to everyone, and highlights the importance of international collaboration in addressing health challenges. The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization (WHO) to Sudan, excluding aid from the UAE, accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from outside the country in 2022, underscoring the UAE's role as a key center for international humanitarian assistance.

translations.source: Kushirikiana ndani ya nchi ni muhimu kwa maendeleo ya kimataifa kuelekea SDGs zinazohusiana na afya Ripoti mpya ya maendeleo inatolewa kwa pamoja leo na mashirika 13 yaliyotia saini mpango wa utekelezaji wa kimataifa wa maisha ya afya na ustawi kwa wote (SDG3 GAP). Ripoti yenye anuani "Ni nini kilifanya kazi? Nini haikufanya? Nini kinafuata?" inaakisi miaka minne ya kazi ya pamoja ya mashirika makubwa ya kimataifa inayoelezea kile ambacho hakijafanya kazi katika kuimarisha ushirikiano na usaidizi ili kuharakisha maendeleo ya nchi kuelekea malengo ya maendeleo endelevu (SDGs) yanayohusiana na afya. Mwaka huu ni ishara ya nusu ya kuelekea SDGs, lakini Dunia ipo nyuma kufikia malengo ya kimataifa. Tangu kuzinduliwa kwake mwaka 2019, SDG3 GAP iliwezesha kuundwa kwa miundo mipya ya ushirikiano kati ya mashirika yaliyotia saini katika maeneo muhimu kama vile ufadhili endelevu na huduma ya afya ya msingi miongoni mwa mengine. Angalau nchi 67 zimejihusisha kwenye moja au zaidi ya maudhui ya kichochezi ya SDG3 GAP. "Tumipiga hatua muhimu, lakini bado tuna njia ndefu ya kusafiri ili kuboresha jinsi mashirika ya kimataifa yanayofanya kazi pamoja kusaidia nchi. Lazima tusikilize kile ambacho nchi zinatuambia na kuchukua hatua kulingana na muongozo wao", alisema Dk Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, mkurugenzi mkuu wa WHO na mwenyekiti wa kundi la wakuu wa SDG3 GAP. "Nawashukuru washirika kwa ushirikiano wao na kwa kujitathmini kwa uaminifu uliomo katika ripoti hii. "Ripoti inatoa mapendekezo sita muhimu ili kuhakikisha kuwa nchi zinanufaika kutokana na usaidizi ulioboreshwa zaidi na kutimiza ahadi zilizotolewa katika SDG3 GAP. Mapendekezo hayo ni pamoja na Kuendeleza mzunguko wa uboreshaji wa SDG3 GAP kwa afya kwa kutafuta maoni ya nchi wanachama kuhusu jinsi tunavyoshirikiana katika ngazi ya nchi na kujibu mapendekezo yanayohusiana; Kudumisha GAP kama jukwaa la ushirikiano; Kukuza ushirikiano wenye nguvu katika ngazi ya chini kuhusu huduma ya afya ya msingi na kuchunguza mada mpya kama vile mifumo ya afya inayostahimili hali ya hewa. Kutumia kwa pamoja mbinu mpya katika ngazi ya nchi kama vile uwasilishaji kwa mbinu ya athari; Kujihusisha zaidi na mashirika ya kiraia; na Kufanya kazi na nchi wanachama ili kuimarisha vitisho vya ushirikiano kupitia uongozi wa kisiasa, mwelekeo wa utawala na ufadhili ili kusaidia ushirikiano. Matokeo muhimu ya ripoti hiyo yatajikita katika majadiliano ya mkutano ujao wa baraza kuu la umoja wa mataifa la SDG na mkutano wa ngazi ya juu kuhusu huduma ya afya kwa wote mnamo Septemba 2023. Miezi kabla ya matukio haya muhimu inatoa fursa kwa msukumo wa pamoja na nchi wanachama na mashirika ya kimataifa kutambua njia za jinsi ya kutekeleza mapendekezo na kuandaa msingi wa kuimarisha ushirikiano wenye nguvu zaidi na kuharakisha maendeleo kuelekea SDGs zinazohusia na afya katika nusu ya pili ya muda wa SDG. Maelezo kwa mhariri: SDG3 GAP inaeleza jinsi mashirika 13 yaliyotia saini yatatumia njia mpya za kufanya kazi, kuendeleza ushirikiano uliopo kwenye mafanikio, na kuoanisha kwa pamoja msaada wao katika mipango na mikakati ya kitaifa ambayo inamilikiwa na kuongozwa na nchi. Waliotia saini SDG3 GAP ni Gavi, Muungano wa Chanjo; Ufadhili wa kituo cha kimataifa kwa wanawake, watoto na vijana (GFF); Mfuko wa kimataifa wa kupambana na UKIMWI, kifua kikuu na Malaria (mfuko wa kimataifa); Shirika la kazi la kimataifa (ILO);
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others. At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP. These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach; engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor: The SDG3 GAP describes how the 13 signatory agencies will adopt new ways of working, building on existing successful collaborations, and jointly align their support around national plans and strategies that are country-owned and -led. The signatories to the SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); International Labour Organization (ILO);
translations.hypothesis: Collaboration within countries is crucial for achieving international development goals related to health. A new report on development, jointly released today by 13 organizations that have signed the international health and well-being partnership agreement (SDG3 GAP), outlines the progress and challenges in achieving these goals. The report, titled "What worked? What didn't? What next?", reflects on four years of collaborative work by major international organizations to strengthen partnerships and support countries in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) related to health. This year marks the halfway point towards achieving the SDGs, but the world is still behind schedule. Since its launch in 2019, SDG3 GAP has enabled the creation of new partnership models between the signatory organizations in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care. At least 67 countries have engaged with one or more of the SDG3 GAP's key areas. "We have made significant progress, but we still have a long way to go to improve the way international organizations work together to support countries. We need to listen to what countries are telling us and take action based on their guidance," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Steering Committee. "I thank our partners for their collaboration and for their honest self-assessment in this report." The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from improved support and achieve the goals outlined in the SDG3 GAP. These recommendations include: Developing a framework for SDG3 GAP implementation in health, involving country members in discussions about how to collaborate at the national level and respond to relevant recommendations; Maintaining the SDG3 GAP as a platform for partnership; Strengthening partnerships at the local level in primary health care and exploring new areas such as climate-resilient health systems; Using new approaches at the national level, such as impact-based financing; Engaging more with civil society organizations; and Working with country members to strengthen partnership through political leadership, governance, and financing. The key findings of the report will be discussed at the upcoming UN General Assembly SDG meeting and the high-level meeting on universal health coverage in September 2023. In the months leading up to these events, there is an opportunity for countries and international organizations to work together to implement the recommendations and build a stronger foundation for achieving the SDGs in the second half of the SDG period. Note to editors: SDG3 GAP outlines how the 13 signatory organizations will use new approaches to working, building on existing successes, and coordinating their support in national plans and strategies owned and led by countries. The signatory organizations to SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; the Global Fund for Women, Children and Youth (GFF); the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); the International Labour Organization (ILO);

translations.source: Mpango wa pamoja wa umoja wa mataifa kuhusu VVU/UKIMWI (UNAIDS); Mpango wa maendeleo wa umoja wa mataifa (UNDP); Shirika la umoja wa mataifa la idadi ya watu (UNFPA); Shirika la umoja wa mataifa la kuhudumia watoto (UNICEF); Unitaid; Shirika la umoja wa mataifa la usawa wa jinsia na uwezeshaji wa wanawake (UN Women); Kundi la Benki ya Dunia Mpango wa chakula Duniani (WFP); na Shirika la afya Duniani (WHO). Tazama ripoti kamili, Nini kilifanya kazi? Nini kinafuata?
