translations.source: Matokeo mapya ya uchunguzi yanaonyesha mifano ya afya ikianza kurejeshwa kutokana na janga Baada ya miaka mitatu ya janga la UVIKO-19, mifumo ya afya katika nchi imeanza kuonyesha dalili kuu za kwanza za kuimarika kwa mfumo wa afya kulingana na ripoti ya muda ya WHO juu ya "Duru ya nne ya uchunguzi wa mapigo ya kimataifa juu ya kuendelea kwa huduma muhimu za afya wakati wa janga la UVIKO-19: Novemba 2022 Januari 2023". Ifikapo mapema mwaka 2023, nchi ziliripoti kukabiliwa na usumbufu mdogo katika utoaji wa huduma za afya za kawaida, lakini zilionyesha haja ya kuwekeza katika kurejeshwa na kustahimili nguvu zaidi kwa siku zijazo. Kati ya nchi 139 zilizojibu duru ya nne ya uchunguzi wa mapigo ya moyo wa WHO, nchi ziliripoti kuendelea kukatika kwa karibu robo moja ya huduma kwa wastani. Katika nchi 84 ambapo uchunguzi wa mwenendo unawezekana, asilimia ya huduma zilizokatizwa ilipungua kwa wstani kutoka 56% mwezi Julai - Septemba 2020 hadi 23% mnamo Novemba 2022 - Januari 2023. Usumbufu unaoendelea unaotokana na mahitaji na upande wa ugavi, ikiwa ni pamoja na viwango vya chini vya watu wanaotafuta huduma za afya katika jamii pamoja na upatikanaji mdogo wa wahudumu wa afya na rasilimali nyingine za afya kama vile zahanati huria au akiba inayopatikana ya dawa na bidhaa. Ni habari njema kwamba mifumo ya afya katika nchi nyingi inaanza kurejesha huduma muhimu za afya kwa mamilioni ya watu ambao hawakuzipata wakati wakati wa janga hili", alisema Dk Rudi Eggers, mkurugenzi wa WHO wa huduma za afya jumuishi. "Lakini tunahitaji kuhakikisha kuwa nchi zote zinaendelea kuziba pengo hili ili kurejesha huduma za afya, na kutumia mafunzo tuliyojifunza ili kujenga mifumo ya afya iliyoandaliwa zaidi na thabiti kwa siku zijazo". Ishara mashuhuri za kwanza za kurejeshwa Katika utafiti huu mpya, nchi chache ziliripoti kwa makusudi kupunguza ufikiaji katika majukwaa yote ya utoaji huduma na kazi muhimu za afya ya umma tangu kuripoti 2020-2021, kuonyesha hatua muhimu ya kurejea viwango vya utoaji wa huduma kabla ya janga na utendaji mpana wa mfumo. Kufikia mwishoni mwa 2022, nchi nyingi ziliripoti dalili za ufufuaji wa huduma, ikiwa ni pamoja katika huduma za afya ya ngono, uzazi, kujifungua, watoto wachanga, watoto na vijana; lishe; chanjo; magonjwa ya kuambukiza (ikijumuisha Malaria, VVU, TB na magonjwa mengine ya zinaa); magonjwa ya kitropiki yaliyopuuzwa; magonjwa yasiyoambukiza; udhibiti wa matatizo ya akili, Neva na matumizi ya dutu; utunzaji wa wazee; na huduma za kitamaduni na/au za ziada. Idadi ya nchi zinazoripoti kukatizwa kwa mfumo wao wa kitaifa wa ugavi imepungua kutoka karibu nusu (nchi 29 kati ya 59 zinazojibu) hadi takribani robo (18 kati ya nchi 66 zinazojibu) mwaka jana. Licha ya dalili za kupona, kukatizwa kwa huduma kunaendelea kaika nchi zote katika kanda na viwango vyote vya mapato, na katika mipangilio mingi ya utoaji wa huduma na maeneo ya huduma za ufuatiliaji. Pia nchi zinakabiliana na ongezeko la mlundikano wa huduma - mara nyingi zaidi katika huduma za uchunguzi, utambuzi na matibabu ya magonjwa yasiyoambukiza - ambayo inaweza kusababisha matokeo mabaya kwani watu wanacheleweshwa kupata huduma kwa wakati. Kurejesha utoaji wa huduma muhimu za afya ni muhimu kwa sababu usumbufu- ikiwemo huduma za kukuza afya, kuzuia magonjwa, uchunguzi, matibabu, urekebishaji na kupooza - kunaweza kuwa na athari mbaya zaidi za kiafya kwa idadi ya watu na kiwango cha mtu binafsi kuliko janga lenyewe, hususani miongoni mwa watu walio hatarini. Kuunganisha huduma za UVIKO-19 katika huduma muhimu za afya katika hatua nyingine muhimu kuelekea ufufuaji na mabadiliko ya mfumo, nchi nyingi zimepiga hatua katika kuunganisha huduma za UVIkO-19 katika utoaji wa huduma za afya za kawaida. Takribani 80-90% ya nchi zimeunganisha kikamilifu huduma za chanjo ya UVIKO-19, uchunguzi na udhibiti wa kesi vilevile huduma za hali baada ya UVIKO-19 katika utoaji wa huduma za kawaida. Bado, nchi nyingi (80% yanchi 83 zinazojibu) ziliripoti angalau kizuizi kimoja cha kuongeza ufikiaji wa zana muhimu za UVIKO-19 (m.f uchunguzi wa UVIKO-19, matibabu, chanjo na vifaa vya kinga ya kibinafsi- PPE), na masuala ya wahudumu wa afya na ukosefu wa fedha unaowakilisha vikwazo vya kawaida. Msaada zaidi unahitajika kwa ajili ya kurejeshwa, uthabiti na kujitayarisha Nchi nyingi zimeanza kutumia yale ambayo wamejifunza kipindi cha janga la UVIKO-19, ikijumuisha kupitia kuweka mikakati kadhaa bunifu ya kupunguza usumbufu wa huduma katika utoaji wa huduma za afya. Hizi ni pamoja na kupelekea mbinu za matibabu ya simu, uhamasishaji wa huduma za nyumbani au uingiliaji wa kujitegemea, mbinu za kuimarisha upatikanaji wa wahudumu wa afya, uwezo na mbinu za usaidizi, ubunifu katika ununuzi na usambazaji wa dawa na vifaa, mawasiliano zaidi ya kawaida ya jamii,n na ushirikiano na watoa huduma na sekta binafsi. Robo tatu ya nchi ziliripoti mgao wa ziada wa ufadhili kuelekea ufufuaji wa mfumo wa muda mrefu, ustahimilivu na utayari. Nchi zilieleza haja ya msaada wa WHO ili kushughulikia changamoto zilizosalia katika muktadha wa UVIKO-19 na zaidi, mara nyingi zinazohusiana na uimarishaji wa wahudumu wa afya, kujenga uwezo wa ufuatiliaji wa huduma za afya, kubuni huduma za afya ya msingi --mfano inayolengwa ya utunzaji, utawala, sera na mipango na mipango ya fedha na ufadhili. Maelezo kwa mhariri: Katika duru ya nne ya uchunguzi wa kimataifa wa Pulse wa WHO, nchi 222, wilaya na maeneo walialikwa kujibu uchunguzi sanifu wa msingi wa mtandao kati ya Novemba 2022 na Januari 2023. Uchunguzi wa nne ulifuatia tafiti za awali za WHO za 2020 na 2021: Duru ya 1(Mei - Septemba 2020), Duru ya 2(Januari - Machi 2021) na Duru ya 3 (Novemva - Desemba 2021) ambao ulionyesha kiwango ambacho janga hilo lilikuwa likiathiri muelekeo wa huduma za afya na jinsi nchi zinavyochukua hatua.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023. Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning. By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery. These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis: The latest survey results show that health systems are beginning to recover from the COVID-19 pandemic. After three years of the pandemic, health systems in countries have begun to show early signs of recovery, according to the WHO's fourth global survey of health system preparedness for COVID-19: November 2022 – January 2023. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing a minor disruption in routine health services, but they also indicated a need to invest in greater resilience and preparedness for the future. Of the 139 countries that responded to the fourth survey, countries reported that approximately one-third of health services were disrupted on average. In 84 countries where data is available, the percentage of services disrupted decreased from an average of 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022 – January 2023. The ongoing disruption is due to a number of factors, including shortages of health workers, supplies, and other health resources, such as free clinics or stockpiles of medications and supplies. It is encouraging to see that health systems in many countries are beginning to restore essential health services to millions of people who were unable to access them during the pandemic", said Dr. Rudi Eggers, WHO Director of Integrated Health Services. "But we need to ensure that all countries continue to address this gap and use the lessons we have learned to build more resilient and sustainable health systems for the future. Key early signs of recovery In this new survey, a few countries reported deliberately reducing access to all health services and essential public health functions since 2020-2021, indicating a significant step back towards pre-pandemic service levels and overall system performance. By the end of 2022, many countries reported signs of recovery in health services, including in sexual and reproductive health, maternal and child health, immunization, infectious diseases (including malaria, HIV, and TB), and non-communicable diseases; nutrition; and mental health. The number of countries reporting disruptions to their national health systems has decreased from almost half (29 out of 59 responding countries) to approximately one-third (18 out of 66 responding countries) last year. Despite the signs of recovery, service disruptions continue in all regions and income levels, and in many service delivery settings and health facilities. Countries are also facing increased demand for services – particularly in non-communicable disease screening, diagnosis, and treatment – which can lead to negative outcomes as people are unable to access care in a timely manner. Restoring essential health services is crucial because disruptions to health services can have more serious health consequences for populations and individuals than the pandemic itself, especially for vulnerable groups. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services is also a key step towards recovery and system transformation. Many countries have taken steps to integrate COVID-19 vaccination, testing, and treatment into routine health services. Approximately 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, testing, and treatment into routine health services. However, many countries (83 out of 83 responding) reported at least one barrier to increasing access to essential COVID-19 tools (including COVID-19 testing, treatment, vaccination, and personal protective equipment), and common constraints include a shortage of health workers and funding. More support is needed to address these challenges. Many countries have begun to use the lessons they have learned from the COVID-19 pandemic, including through a number of innovative strategies to reduce service disruptions in health service delivery. These include using remote consultation, home-based care, and self-management approaches, strengthening health worker capacity, and using innovative supply chain and distribution methods. Three-quarters of countries reported increased investment in long-term system recovery, resilience, and preparedness. Countries have also expressed a need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, including strengthening health workforce, improving health service monitoring, and designing primary health care services – including targeted care, governance, policies, and planning – and mobilizing financial and technical resources.

translations.source: Ilhali tafiti za Pulse zina vikwazo kama vile kuripoti upendeleo na uwakilishi, nguvu ya juhudi hii ni ya kina na inatoa taarifa kwa haraka. Neno "nchi" linapaswa kueleweka kujumuisha nchi, wilaya na maeneo yote. Uchunguzi wa mienendo ulikamilishwa kulingana na majibu kutoka nchi, wilaya au maeneo 84 ambayo yalijibu angalau sehemu moja ya utafiti katika duru zote nne za uchunguzi wa Pulse.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis: Despite the limitations of the Pulse surveys, such as reporting bias and representation issues, this study provides in-depth information and quick results. The term "country" should be understood to include all countries, regions, and areas. The analysis was completed based on responses from 84 countries, regions, or areas that responded to at least one part of the Pulse survey in all four rounds of the study.

translations.source: Taarifa ya kusherehekea maadhimisho ya miaka 50 ya HRP: Swideni na Uingereza na Ireland kaskazini Ili kukamata ari ya wajumbe wa Kamati ya Sera na Uratibu (PCC) wanaosherehekea miaka 50 ya HRP na mafanikio yake mengi, Sweden na Uingereza na Ireland Kaskazini walisoma maelezo yafuatayo ya shukurani na pongezi katika mkutano wa 36 wa HRP PCC. Kizazi chetu kimeshuhudia maendeleo zaidi katika sayansi na teknolojia kuliko vizazi vyote vilivyotangulia kwa pamoja na kushuhudia mabadiliko kadhaa ya kiakili, kiteknolojia, kiafya na kijamii yakiongozwa na mapinduzi ya uzazi wa mpango na kufuatiwa na maendeleo ya afya ya uzazi,ubunifu wa afya vilevile haki na usawa wa kijinsia. Hata hivyo, changamoto kubwa katika uga wa SRHR zinaendelea, na, bilashaka, baadhi ya maendeleo leo yanahojiwa tena. Tunawapongeza wafanyakazi wa HRP, washirika na waungaji mkono kwa mafanikio mengi hadi sasa na kutambua hatua kubwa zilizofikiwa katika nusu karne katika kutoa ushahidi unaounga mkono upatikanaji kamili wa usawa wa afya na haki za ngono na uzazi. Tungependa kutaja makundi makuu matatu ya mafanikio: Kwanza, utafiti wa ubora wa juu wa HRP umekuwa na athari inayoonekana kwenye sera ya afya ya umma, viwango, na mazoezi Dunianikote kushughulikia mahitaji yanayoendelea ya watu mbalimbali. Pili,HRP, hasa kupitia muungano wa HRP, imeimarisha taasisi za utafii ndani na nje ya nchi, na imewezesha watafiti wengi kupata ujuzi wa utafiti ambao unahakikisha kwamba utafiti wa ubora wa juu wa SRHR unaweza kuendelea, na kwa hakika kuongezeka, katika siku zijazo. Tatu, katika Dunia iliyokumbwa na matatizo, migogoro na kuongezeka upinzani dhidi ya usawa wa kijinsia, HRP imewezesha uzingatiaji wa haki za kimataifa za wanawake na wasichana na watu walio katika mazingira magumu. HRP inasalia kuwa chombo kikuu ndani ya mfumo wa umoja wa mataifa kwa ajili utafiti katika uzazi wa Binadamu na inaendelea kuwa kiongozi wa kipekee katika afya ya ngono na uzazi Duniani. HRP na washirika wake wametuleta karibu na ahadi ya mkutano wa kimataifa wa idadi ya watu na maendeleo wa 1994 na wito wake wa kuweka hali za watu binafsi, hususani wanawake na wasichana, katika mhimili wa maendeleo endelevu. Tunataka kutoa pongezi na shukurani zetu kwa sekretarieti ya HRP, washirika wake, wafadhili wenza na mtandao wa kimataifa wa vituo shirikishi na wataalamu kusaidia kuhakikisha juhudi zake zinachangia kuleta mabadiliko kwa wanawake, wanaume, watoto, vijana na jamii mbalimabali HRP imejitolea kuhudumia. Kwa niaba ya wanachama wa PCC, turuhusu kuthibitisha ushirikiano wetu nanyi ili kuunga mkono mafanikio ya HRP, ambayo mafanikio ya ajenda ya 2030 inategemea.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights. We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health. HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.
translations.hypothesis: Commemoration of the 50th Anniversary of HRP: Sweden, the United Kingdom, and Northern Ireland – Celebrating the achievements of the HRP Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members as they mark the 50th anniversary of HRP and its many successes, Sweden and the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland reflected on the following expressions of gratitude and congratulations at the 36th HRP PCC meeting. Our generation has witnessed more advancements in science and technology than any previous generation, and we have also seen significant changes in the social, technological, and health spheres, driven by the demographic transition and followed by advances in reproductive health, family planning, and gender equality. However, significant challenges in the field of SRHR persist, and some of the progress made to date is now being questioned. We commend the HRP staff, partners, and supporters for their many achievements to date and recognize the significant progress that has been made over the past half-century in providing evidence to support the full realization of health and sexual and reproductive rights. We would like to highlight three key areas of achievement: First, HRP's high-quality research has had a demonstrable impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide, helping to address the evolving needs of diverse populations. Second, HRP, particularly through the HRP network, has strengthened research institutions both domestically and internationally, enabling many researchers to gain the skills and knowledge necessary to ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue and even expand in the future. Third, in a world beset by conflict, inequality, and rising opposition to gender equality, HRP has played a crucial role in promoting the international human rights of women and girls and those living in difficult circumstances. HRP remains a vital component of the UN system for research on population and development, and it continues to be a leading voice in SRHR worldwide. HRP and its partners have been instrumental in bringing the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to place the needs of people, particularly women and girls, at the center of sustainable development, to fruition. We would like to express our gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, funding agencies, and the international network of collaborating institutions and experts for their support in ensuring that HRP's efforts contribute to positive change for women, men, children, young people, and communities. On behalf of the PCC members, we would like to reaffirm our commitment to working with you to support the achievements of HRP, which are essential to the success of the 2030 Agenda.

translations.source: WHO inazindua mpango mpya wa kuboresha utayari wa janga Ili kusaidia nchi kujiandaa vyema kwa magonjwa ya mlipuko ya siku sijazo, WHO imezindua mpango mpya leo ambao unatoa muongozo kuhusu upangaji jumuishi wa kukabiliana na vimelea vyovyote vya upumuaji kama vile mafua au virusi vya Korona. Mpango mpya wa kujitayarisha na ustahimilivu kwa mpango wa vitisho vinavyoibuka, au PRET, inajmuisha zana na mbinu za hivi punde zaidi za kujifunza pamoja na kuchukua hatua za pamoja zilizoainishwa wakati wa janga la UVIKO-19 na dharura nyingine za hivi karibuni za afya ya Umma. Kupitia mpango huo, WHO itatumia mbinu ya maambukizi kuziongoza nchi katika upangaji wa janga hilo, ikizingatiwa kwamba nafasi na uwezo mkubwa ni wa wakaida miongoni mwa makundi ya vimelea vya magonjwa. PRET inajibu mwito wa muongozo za kiufundi na usaidizi kwa ajili ya kukuza na kuimarisha utayari jumuishi na muitikio, kama ilivyoainishwa katika maazimio ya baraza la afya ulimwenguni. Janga la UVIKO-19 na dharura nyingine za kiafya zimeonyesha kuwa nchi zinahitaji kuwa tayari kukabiliana na matishio ya magonjwa ya kuambukiza, na mipango mahususi ya kujitayarisha mikononi na uratibu bora na ushirikiano na sekta nyingine kama vile kilimo. "Shughuli za kujitayarisha, kuzuia, na kukabiliana hazipaswi kuwa eneo la sekta ya afya pekee", alisema Dk Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, mkurugenzi mkuu wa WHO. "Kama vile hali za dharura za kiafya zinavyoathiri katika sekta nyingi, ndivyo pia juhudi zetu za kujitayarisha na kukabiliana nazo zienee katika sekta, taaluma na viini vya magonjwa. Pia ni muhimu kwamba ushirikishwaji wa jamii na usawa ni kitovu cha jamii zetu, hususani kwa wale watu waliotengwa na walio hatarini zaidi". PRET ni mageuzi katika mkabala wa WHO wa kujitayarisha kwa janga kupitia utumiaji wa lenzi ya maambukizi, badala ya kuzingatia magonjwa mahususi. WHO itaendelea kuandaa na kusambaza muongozo kuhusu magonjwa maalumu inapohitajika. Uzinduzi wa mpango huo unafanyika katika siku ya mwisho ya mikutano na zaidi ya watu 120 wanaowakilisha nchi wanachama, washirika na sekretarieti ya WHO, kufafanua mustakabali ujao wa PRET, ikijumuisha maendeleo ya mpango wa utekelezaji wa kimataifa kwa ajili ya maandalizi ya janga la vimelea. Sehemu ya kwanza ya mpango wa PRET inaangazia vimelea vya magonjwa ya kupumua, ikiwa ni pamojana mafua, virusi vya Korona, virusi vya kupumua vya syncytial. Kwa kuzingatia janga la UVIKO-19 linaloendelea na tishio linalowezekana la mafua ya ndege, sehemu hii itawezesha nchi kukagua, kupima na kusasisha juhudi zao za kupanga janga la kupumua ili kuhakikisha kuwa zina nafasi na uwezo wa kufanya kazi. Mchakato unaendelea ili kutambua kundi la linalofuata la vimelea vya magonjwa, kama vile Arboviruses, kushughulikiwa chini ya mpango huu. Hii itafuata vipaumbele vilivyoainishwa kupitia mapendekezo kumi ya kuimarisha usanifu wa kimataifa kwa ajili ya maandalizi ya dharura ya afya, majibu, na ustahimilivu (HEPR). Mpango wa PRET unaoleta enzi mpya ya kujitayarisha kwa janga na inawakilisha mageuzi ya shughuli za msingi za WHO ili kusaidia nchi wanachama wote katika kuimarisha utayari wa dharura wa afya, kuzuia, na kukabiliana na nafasi na uwezo. Pia mpango huo unaweza kutumika kutekeleza malengo na masharti ya makubaliano ya janga la ugonjwa ambao kwa sasa unajadiliwa na nchi wanachama wa WHO. Kufuatia mijadala na mazungumzo miongoni mwa washiriki wa mkutano, WHO inawaalika washikadau kwenye wito huu wa kimataifa kuchukua hatua ili kuendeleza utyarishaji wa vimelea vya magonjwa ya kupumua. Wito kwa hatua. WHO, nchi wanachama, na washirika wanafanya kazi pamoja kutambua na kutekeleza hatua za kipaumbele ili kuimarisha utayari wa kitaifa, kikanda na kimataifa kwa magonjwa ya milipuko na matishio yanayoibuka ya magonjwa ya kuambukiza. Tunatambua jukumu la jamii na sekta zote, na haja ya kuimarishwa na kuendelea kwa kasi ili kumaliza mzunguko wa hofu na kutekelezwa ambao ni wa kawaida sana katika kujiandaa na kukabiliana na janga. Tunakumbuka mafunzo kutoka kwa majanga na magonjwa ya milipuko ya hapo awali, hususani yale yanayosababishwa na magonjwa ya kupumua. Vimelea hivi vina na vitaendelea kuwa tishio kubwa la kimataifa lenye uwezekano wa kusababisha magonjwa na vifo vingi, kuelemea mifumo ya afya, na kudhoofisha uchumi wa Dunia, na kuzidisha ukosefu wa usawa, ambao upo katika upatikanaji wa zana zote mbili za kuzuia magonjwa ya mlipuko na huduma za afya kwa watu wote. Huu ni wito wa kuchukua hatua ili kuharakisha kujitayarisha kwa magonjwa ya mlipuko na vitisho vinavyoibuka Duniani.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases. WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative. This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
translations.hypothesis: WHO launches new plan to improve pandemic preparedness to help countries better prepare for future outbreaks. Today, WHO launched a new plan that provides guidance on comprehensive pandemic preparedness, including how to respond to a wide range of infectious diseases, such as influenza or coronavirus. The new Pandemic and Emergency Threat Preparedness (PRET) plan includes the latest tools and strategies for learning and taking coordinated action during a pandemic, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through this plan, WHO will use a "one health" approach to guide countries in pandemic preparedness, recognizing that the greatest risks are associated with zoonotic diseases. PRET responds to the call for technical guidance and support to strengthen and enhance pandemic preparedness and response, as outlined in the World Health Assembly resolutions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be prepared to respond to the threat of infectious diseases, and that specific preparedness plans are essential. PRET is a shift in WHO's pandemic preparedness approach, focusing on a "one health" approach rather than specific diseases. WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of this plan takes place on the final day of the meeting and more than 120 representatives from member states, partners, and WHO Secretariat are attending, to discuss the future of PRET, including the development of an international implementation plan for pandemic preparedness. The first phase of the PRET plan focuses on respiratory infectious diseases, including influenza, coronavirus, and other respiratory viruses. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the potential for a future influenza pandemic, this phase will enable countries to assess, test, and update their pandemic preparedness plans to ensure they have the capacity to respond. The process will continue to identify and address other respiratory infectious diseases, such as arboviruses. This will follow the priorities outlined in the ten recommendations for strengthening the international health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR) architecture. The PRET plan represents a new era of pandemic preparedness and is a shift in WHO's core activities to support all member states in strengthening their health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capabilities. The plan can also be used to implement the goals and objectives of the pandemic treaty, which is currently being discussed by WHO member states. Following discussions and consultations among participants, WHO invites stakeholders to take action to strengthen pandemic preparedness for respiratory infectious diseases.

translations.source: Kujiandaa kwa ufanisi kunategemea mipango thabiti na hatua zilizoratibiwa. Ahadi ya pamoja ya hatua zifuatazo itaona maendeleo yaliyofikiwa ifikapo Desemba 2025 kama yatakavyo fafanuliwa katika mfumo wa ufuatiliaji wa (kujiandaa na ustahimilivu kwa vitisho vinavyojitokeza (PRET): Kusasisha mipango ya kujitayarisha ambayo inathibitisha vitendo vya kipaumbele na ambavyo imezingatia mafunzo kutoka kwa matukio ya zamani. Kutambua hatari inayoletwa na vimelea vya magonjwa ya kupumua, kupanga janga la vimelea vya upumuaji kwa kuzingatia mada zilizoainishwa katika Moduli ya PRET #1. Kupanga kipaumbele kwa magonjwa ya vimelea vya upumuaji Kuongeza muunganisho kati ya washikadau katika upangaji maandalizi ya janga kupitia uratibu na ushirikiano wa kimfumo. Hii inajumuisha kujenga mifumo yenye usawa; kufanya mazoezi ya viungo; Na kushiriki taarifa kuhusu mazoea mazuri, changamoto na fursa. Kujitolea kwa uwekezaji endelevu, ufadhili na ufuatiliaji wa utayari wa janga kwa kuzingatia mapengo yaliyo ainishwa wakati w magonjwa ya mlipuko na milipuko iliyopita. Muitikio wa janga la UVIKO-19 umeonyesha kile kinachoweza kufikiwa kwa kujitolea kisiasa, ushirikishwaji wa jamii na ufadhili. Kiini cha kazi hii ni kuhakikisha usawa kuwa tayari kwa janga lijalo pamoja. Hatua ya jamuii nzima inahitajika ili kufanya maendeleo yaliyoainishwa katika wito huu wa hatua. Utekelezaji unapaswa kuimarisha uimara wa jamii, kudumisha , kuendeleza, na kujenga juu ya mifumo ya kawaida; na kuongeza uwezo mpana zaidi wa kujiandaa na kukabiliana na Dharura. Wito huu wa kuchukua hatua ulitolewa katika maandalizi na ustahimilivu kwa vitisho vinavyoibuka (PRET): mkutano wa kimataifa wa magonjwa ya vimelea vya upumuaji ya baadae uliofanyika tarehe 24-26 Aprili 2023 huko Jeniva, Uswisi.
translations.reference: Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding. At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis: Effective preparedness relies on well-defined plans and pre-arranged steps. The following commitments to the next steps will demonstrate progress towards achieving the goals outlined by December 2025, as detailed in the preparedness and resilience framework (PRET): Updating preparedness plans that reflect prioritized actions and incorporate lessons learned from past events. Identifying and mitigating the risks posed by respiratory infectious diseases, and developing a pandemic preparedness plan based on the topics outlined in PRET Module #1. Prioritizing preparedness for respiratory infectious diseases. Enhancing coordination among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness through systematic coordination and collaboration. This includes establishing balanced systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on best practices, challenges, and opportunities. Committing to sustainable investment, funding, and monitoring of pandemic preparedness, taking into account the gaps identified in past outbreaks and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic has demonstrated what can be achieved through political commitment, community engagement, and funding. The core of this work is to ensure that we are prepared for the next pandemic together. A collective effort is needed to achieve the goals outlined in this call to action. Implementation should strengthen community resilience, maintain, develop, and build upon existing systems; and enhance broader preparedness and response capabilities. This call to action was issued during the preparedness and resilience framework (PRET): International Conference on Post-Ebola Respiratory Diseases, held on April 24-26, 2023, in Geneva, Switzerland.

translations.source: WHO inapendekeza kuongezeka kwa utekelezaji wa zana zinazopendekezwa za kukabiliana na Malaria Kuadhimisha siku ya Malaria Duniani, shirika la afya Duniani (WHO) linatoa wito wa kuongezwa kwa utekelezaji wa afua mpya na zilizopo ili kuokoa misha kutokana na Malaria. Karibu watoto milioni 1.5 walio katika hatari kubwa ya ugonjwa na kifo kutokana na Malaria nchini Ghana, Kenya na Malawi sasa wamepokea dozi yao ya kwanza ya chanjo ya kwanza ya Malaria, RTS,S/AS 01 (RTS,S), shukrani kwa mpango unaoendelea wa majaribio unaoratibiwa na WHO. Majaribio ya chanjo ya Malaria, ilyozinduliwa mwaka 2019, inaongeza usawa katika ufikiaji wa kuzuia Malaria kwa walio hatarini zaidi na wanaookoa maisha. Kama itatekelezwa kwa mapana, WHO inakadiria kuwa chanjo ya Malaria inaweza kuokoa maisha ya makumi kwa maelfu ya watoto kila mwaka. "Tunazo zana za kukabiliana na ugonjwa wa Malaria, kifurushi cha afua ambacho kinajumuisha udhibiti wa Vekta, Dawa za kuzuia, upimaji na matibabu", alisema Dk Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, mkurugenzi mkuu wa WHO. "Hawa wameunganishwa na chanjo salama na yenye ufanisi ya Malaria, ambayo, inaweza kuokoa maisha ya makumi ya maelfu ya watoto kila mwaka. Kwa uwekezaji endelevu na juhudi zilizoongezwa kufikia walio hatarini zaidi, kutokomeza Malaria katika nchi nyingi kunawezekana. "Siku ya Malaria Duniani 2023 inaadhimisha chini ya kauli mbiu "wakati wa kutokomeza Malaria: Wekeza, Vumbua, tekeleza". Ndani ya Mada hii, WHO inahimiza utekelezaji bora zaidi wa zana na mikakati iliyopo ya kuzuia, kuchunguza na kutibu Malaria, hasa miongoni mwa watu waliotengwa. Kulingana na ripoti ya hivi karibuni ya ugonjwa wa Malaria Duniani, iliyochapishwa mnamo Desemba 2022, kulikuwa na visa vipya milioni 247 vya Malaria mwaka 2021. WHO kanda ya Afrika inaendelea kubeba mzigo mzito zaidi wa ugonjwa huo ikichukua wastani wa 95% ya visa vyote vya Malaria (milioni 234) na 96% ya vifo vote (593,000) mnamo 2021. Karibu 80% ya vifo vya Malaria katika kanda ya Afrika vilikuwa miongoni mwa watoto chini ya umri wa miaka mitano. Mikakati na zana mpya Nchi zimepiga hatua katika kupanua upatikanaji wa huduma za Malaria kwa watu walio katika hatari zaidi. Licha ya mafanikio fulani, watu wengi walio katika hatari kubwa ya Malaria bado wanakosa huduma zinazoweza kuzuia, kugundua na kutibu ugonjwa huo. Changamoto katika kupanua upatikanaji wa huduma za Malaria zimeongezeka, hususani huko Afrika kusini mwa jangwa la Sahara, janga la UVIKO-19, kuunganisha migogoro ya kibinadamu, vikwazo vya fedha, mifumo dhaifu ya ufuatiliaji, na kupungua kwa ufanisi wa zana kuu za kupambana na malaria. Kushughulikia matishio haya na kusaidia nchi katika kujenga programu zinazostahimili ugonjwa wa Malaria, WHO hivi karibuni ilichapisha mikakati na mifumo mipya, ikijumuisha: Mkakati mpya wa kudhibiti ukinzani wa dawa za Malaria barani Afrika; Mpango mpya wa kukomesha kuenea kwa Anopheles stephensi katika mazingira ya mijini; Mfumo mpya, ulioandaliwa kwa pamoja na WHO na UN-Habitat, ili kuwaongoza viongozi wa majiji katika udhibiti wa Malaria mijini. Zana mpya za kusaisdia nchi kutathmini mifumo yao ya uchunguzi wa Malaria na kutambua maeneo ya uwekezaji Pia WHO imeongeza uwazi, kubadilika na kufikia mapendekezo yake ya Malaria. Miongozo iliyounganishwa na WHO kuhusu Malaria sasa inapatikana kupitia majukwaa mawili ya kidijitaali: MAGICapp na programu ya "Malaria Toolkit". WHO inazihimiza nchi kurekebisha mapendekezo kulingana na mipangilio ya magonjwa ya eneo hilo kwa athari kubwa zaidi.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year. “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations. According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations. Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments; a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.
translations.hypothesis: WHO recommends increased implementation of existing and new tools to combat malaria. Approximately 1.5 million children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi are at high risk of contracting the disease and dying from it.
